1: vayehi dvar-YHWH elai bishnat hateshiit bachodesh haasiri beahor lachodesh lemor (wayhî dĕbar-Yhwh ʾēlay bišnat hattĕšîʿît baḥōdeš hāʿăśîrî bĕʾeḥād laḥōdeš lēmōr): The precise date — ninth year, tenth month, tenth day — is one of Ezekiel's six exact dates (1:2; 8:1; 20:1; 24:1; 26:1; 29:1). This date corresponds to January 588 BCE and aligns with 2 Kings 25:1 and Jer 52:4: the day Nebuchadnezzar's siege of Jerusalem began. Ezekiel in Babylon receives the word on the exact day the siege begins in Jerusalem — a demonstration of prophetic foreknowledge and YHWH's sovereign coordination of events across 800 miles.
2: ben-adam ketov lecha et-shem hayom et-etzem hayom hazeh samach melekh-Bavel el-Yerushalayim beetzem hayom hazeh (ben-ʾādām kĕtōb lĕkā ʾet-šem hayyôm ʾet-ʾeṣem hayyôm hazzeh): Ketov lecha (write down for yourself) — the command to record the date creates a prophetic document of synchrony. Etzem hayom hazeh (this very day / the bone of this day) — the word etzem (bone/substance) intensifies the present moment: this is not an approximate or symbolic date but the actual day of siege-commencement. The prophetic act of recording the date is itself a theological statement: YHWH knows the precise moment and has planned around it.
3: umashol el-beit hameri mashal veamarta alayhem ko amar Adonai YHWH shefot hasir shefot vegam-yeqeh mavim aleyhu (ûmšōl ʾel-bêt hammerî māšāl wĕʾāmartā ʾălêhem kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh šĕpōṭ-hassîr šĕpōṭ): Mashal (parable/allegory) — the parable of the cooking pot (sir) is YHWH's command to Ezekiel. Beit hameri (rebellious house) — this is Ezekiel's standard epithet for Israel (occurring in chs. 2-3, 12, 17, 24, 44). The pot-parable reverses the image the Jerusalem leaders were using as self-reassurance (Ezek 11:3: “this city is the pot and we are the meat” — meaning the pot protects the contents). Ezekiel accepts their metaphor but redefines it: yes, Jerusalem is a pot — but one filled with filth and scum, not a protective vessel.
4: esof netacheha eliyah kol-netach tov yarekh vekhatef male atzamot mivchar (ʾĕsōp̄ nĕtāḥêhā ʾēlêhā kol-nētaḥ ṭôb yārēk wĕkātēp̄ mālēʾ ʿăṣāmôt mibḥār): Netach tov (good piece/choice cut) — in Ezek 11:3 the city's inhabitants thought they were the basar tov (good meat) protected by the pot. Now YHWH takes up the image: yes, put in the good pieces — but it is the quality of the meat that makes the filth worse. The choicer the contents, the greater the tragedy of the pot's corruption. Jerusalem's privileged covenant status makes her unfaithfulness more, not less, culpable.
5: mivchar hatson qach vegam daur haatzamot tachateyha rachob rachoba vegam bashel atzameyha betokha (mibḥar haṣṣōʾn qāḥ wĕgam dûr hāʿăṣāmôt taḥtêhā rabbōt rabbôt wĕgam bašṣēl ʿăṣāmêhā bĕtôkāh): Mivchar hatson (choicest of the flock) — only the best is used. The piling up of bones beneath intensifies the heat. The image of boiling until even the bones soften and dissolve within the pot anticipates the total destruction (v. 10: “let the bones be burned”). Nothing protective will remain — the pot will destroy what it contains rather than preserve it.
6: lachen ko amar Adonai YHWH oi ir hadamim sir asher chelata vo vechelata lo yatza mimenu befeluteha hotsia-uha lo-nafal aleyha goral (lākēn kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh ʾôy ʿîr haddāmîm sir ʾăšer ḥelʾātāh bāh wĕḥelʾātāh lōʾ yāṣĕʾāh mimmennāh): Helah (חֶלְאָה, rust/scum/filth) is a rare word appearing only in this chapter (vv. 6, 11, 12). It refers to the metallic scum, rust, or crust that forms on bronze pots and cannot be removed even by heat and scrubbing — used here as a metaphor for Jerusalem's indelible moral corruption. Ir hadamim (city of blood) — the combination of indelible filth and blood makes Jerusalem a doubly condemned city: morally corrupt and blood-guilty.
7: ki dama betokha haya al-tsur hachalaq samatu lo shefachu al-haarets lekhasot aleyhu afar (kî dāmāh bĕtôkāh hāyāh ʿal-ṣûr heḥālāq śāmāttû lōʾ šĕpāḥûhû ʿal-hāʾāreṣ lĕkassôt ʿālāw ʿāpār): Al-tsur hachalaq (on a bare/smooth rock) — the uncovering of blood is the intensification of guilt. Leviticus 17:13 requires blood from slaughtered animals to be covered with dust; uncovered blood cries out from the ground (Gen 4:10). Jerusalem's blood is exposed on bare rock — it cannot be absorbed, cannot be covered, cannot be silenced. It cries out permanently for judgment.
8: lehavot chemah lingom niqmah natati et-dama al-tsur hachalaq lebilti hiyasotou (lĕhaʿălôt ḥēmāh lĕhinqqōm nāqām nātatî ʾet-dāmāh ʿal-ṣûr heḥālāq lĕbiltî hikkāsôt): Lehavot chemah (to stir up fury/wrath) — YHWH deliberately does not cover the blood precisely so that it will generate the wrath required for justice. The exposed blood is not an oversight but a divine choice: uncovered blood will cry until justice answers. The same principle operates in Abel's blood (Gen 4:10), in the martyrs under the altar (Rev 6:9-10) who cry “how long?” until the Day of wrath.
9: lachen ko amar Adonai YHWH oi ir hadamim gam-ani agdil et-hamedura (lākēn kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh ʾôy ʿîr haddāmîm gam-ʾănî ʾagdîl ʾet-hamĕdûrâ): The doubled ir hadamim (vv. 6, 9) forms an inclusio around the first portion of the pot-oracle. YHWH now takes the role of cook: “I also will make the pile of fuel great.” The judge himself stokes the fire. The divine first-person involvement in the judgment (gam-ani, “even I”) signals that this is no ordinary military defeat but YHWH's own judicial action.
10: harbeh haetsim hadleq haesh hatem habasar veharqach hammizgah vehaetzamot yisarefu (harbēh hāʿēṣîm hadlēq hāʾēš hatem habbāśār wĕhārqōaḥ hammizgāh wĕhāʿăṣāmôt yiśśārēp̄û): Raqach (רֶקַח, spices/seasoning) — even the spices are added, making the cooking thorough and complete. But the goal is not a meal; it is total consumption. Vehaetzamot yisarefu (let the bones be burned) — the burning of bones is the most complete form of destruction in the ancient world; nothing remains. This is the imagery of 586 BCE enacted in symbol: Jerusalem will be as thoroughly destroyed as a pot boiled until the bones themselves calcify and burn.
11: veha-amideyha al-gechaleha reqah lema-an yicham venichara nechoshta vetamach betokha tumata visaref chelata (wĕhāʿĕmîdêhā ʿal-gĕḥālêhā rēqāh lĕmaʿan yiḥam wĕniḥar nĕḥoštāh wĕnittĕkāh bĕtôkāh ṭumʾātāh wĕtiśśārēp̄ ḥelʾātāh): Tumata vesaref chelata (its uncleanness/filthiness melts and its scum is burned) — the double purification: heat to melt the moral filth, fire to burn the scum. The theology of purgative judgment: not destruction for its own sake but the burning-away of what cannot be removed any other way. The pot is set on empty coals — not cooking contents, but burning itself.
12: leat heluateha tokhlot veloo-yetze mamenna chelata-ha rabbah baesh chelata (lĕʾōt hĕlʾātāh hôgāʿattāh wĕlōʾ-yēṣēʾ mimmennāh ḥelʾātāh hārabbāh bāʾēš ḥelʾātāh): Heluateha (her toil/wearying) — Jerusalem has worn herself out with “deception” or “lying effort” (tavlah = falsehood/deception). Despite all the heat and effort, the great scum (chelata harabba) will not come out. The imagery diagnoses the pastoral reality: some corruption is so deep-seated that it cannot be removed by gradual reform — only radical judgment will cleanse it.
13: betumtekh zimma yaan tiharteykhi velo tahart mitumtekhi lo titaheri od ad haniachi et-chatati vekhi (bĕṭumʾātēk zimmāh yaʿan ṭihartîk wĕlōʾ ṭāhārt miṭumʾātēk lōʾ tiṭhărî ʿôd ʿad hănîḥî ʾet-ḥēmātî bāk): Betumtekh zimma (in your filthiness/impurity is lewdness) — the filth has become structural; it is not a surface contamination but the substance of the city's identity. Tiharteykhi velo tahart (I tried to cleanse you and you would not be cleansed) — YHWH's prior attempts at purification (prophets, exiles, warnings) were insufficient because the zimmah penetrated too deep. Only the full expenditure of divine wrath (ad haniachi et-chatati) will accomplish what reformers could not.
14: ani YHWH dibarti baa veasiti lo efra velo achus velo einachem kedrakeyikh ukealeyleikh shafetu otach neum Adonai YHWH (ʾănî Yhwh dibbartî bāʾāh wĕʿāśîtî lōʾ ʾepraʿ wĕlōʾ-ʾāḥûs wĕlōʾ ʾinnāḥēm kĕdĕrākayik ûkĕʿălîlôtayik šĕpāṭûk nĕʾum ʾădōnāy Yhwh): Three negatives: lo efra (will not relent/let loose), lo achus (will not spare/show pity), lo einachem (will not repent/relent). The triple negative is YHWH's most absolute statement of irrevocable judgment in Ezekiel. The judgment has moved beyond the point of intercession or reversal. Jeremiah had been told similarly not to pray for this people (Jer 7:16; 11:14; 14:11). The divine compassion that normally stays the hand of judgment is here explicitly set aside because the sin has reached its full measure.
15: vayehi devar-YHWH elai lemor (wayhî dĕbar-Yhwh ʾēlay lēmōr): The divine-word formula interrupts the cooking-pot oracle to introduce the sign of Ezekiel's wife's death. The structural placement is deliberate: immediately after YHWH's declaration of absolute irrevocable judgment (v. 14), the most personal cost of prophecy is revealed. The prophet who must declare judgment without pity must himself experience loss without public mourning.
16: ben-adam hineni lokeach mimeykh et-machmad einekha bemaggefa veloh-tispod veloh-tivkeh veloh-tavo dimateykha (ben-ʾādām hinnênî lōqēaḥ mimmĕkā ʾet-maḥmad ʿênêkā bĕmaggēp̄â wĕlōʾ-tispōd wĕlōʾ-tibkeh wĕlōʾ-tābôʾ dimʿātekā): Maḥmad ʿênêkā (מַחְמַד עֵינֶיךָ, delight/desire of your eyes) — the most tender epithet for Ezekiel's wife, occurring nowhere else in this form. The same phrase is applied to the temple in v. 21 (“the pride of your strength, the desire of your eyes”), making the wife a type of the temple. Ezekiel's personal loss mirrors Israel's coming loss: as he loses what he most loves, so Israel will lose what she most values. The prophet's body becomes a living prophecy (sign-act).
17: dom heanek metim avl lo-taase paarcha al-roshekha al-shuv venal-eykha tasse velo tateh el-sefam velechem anashim lo tochel (dōm hēʾānēq mētîm ʾēbel lōʾ-taʿăśeh pĕʾērĕkā ḥăbōš ʿālêkā wĕnaʿălĕkā taśîm bĕraglêkā wĕlōʾ tate ʾel-śāpām wĕleḥem ʾănāšîm lōʾ tōʾkal): Dom heanek (sigh quietly/groan in silence) — Ezekiel is permitted only suppressed grief, not public mourning. The prohibited mourning rites: not covering the lip (sefam — a sign of lament and uncleanness, Lev 13:45; Mic 3:7), not eating the leḥem anashim (bread of mourners — food brought by community to comfort the bereaved, lit. “bread of men/condolence food”). These prohibitions reverse the normal social support for grief, isolating Ezekiel as a sign of the communal devastation to come when Israel will be too overwhelmed to mourn properly.
18: vadaber el-haam baboker vatamot ishti baarev va-ase baboker ka-asher tsuveti (wāʾădabbēr ʾel-hāʿam babbōqer wattāmot ʾištî bāʿereb wāʾaʿaś babbōqer kaʾăšer ṣûwwêtî): The verse moves with devastating brevity: “I spoke to the people in the morning. In the evening my wife died. The next morning I did as I was commanded.” The emotional compression is itself prophetically significant — the prophet's obedience is total, extending to the most private sphere of his life. There is no gap between divine command and prophetic execution, even when the cost is unbearable.
19: vayomeru elai haam halo-tagid lanu mah-eleh lanu ki asah khen (wayyōʾmĕrû ʾēlay hāʿām hăl-ōʾ-taggîd lānû mah-ʾēlleh lānû kî ʿōśeh kōhen): The people's question (mah-eleh lanu, “what do these things mean for us?”) is precisely what sign-acts are designed to provoke. The community understands that Ezekiel's unusual behavior is a communicative act — a mofet (sign) — and seeks the interpretation. This is the hermeneutical function of prophetic embodiment: to create curiosity and readiness for the word of interpretation.
20: vaomar alayhem dvar-YHWH hayah elai lemor (wāʾōmar ʾălêhem dĕbar-Yhwh hāyāh ʾēlay lēmōr): The divine-word reception formula is Ezekiel's standard authorization marker. The insertion of this formula in the midst of the narrative (between the question and the answer) emphasizes that what follows is not Ezekiel's personal explanation but a divinely authorized interpretation. The prophet is not performing a creative act of self-expression; he is transmitting the meaning YHWH attached to the sign.
21: emor leveit Yisrael ko amar Adonai YHWH hineni mechalel et-miqdashi geyon uzekhem machmad einekhem umachmad naphsheykhem uvneyikhem uvnoteyikhem asher azavtem bacherev yipolu (ʾĕmōr lĕbêt yiśrāʾēl kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh hinnênî mĕḥallēl ʾet-miqdāšî gĕʾôn ʿuzzĕkem maḥmad ʿênêkem): Geyon uzekhem (the pride of your strength) — the temple is Jerusalem's identity-anchor: the source of national pride, the guarantee of divine protection. Machmad einekhem umachmad naphsheykhem (the desire of your eyes, the longing of your souls) — the same word used for Ezekiel's wife (v. 16). YHWH will profane his own sanctuary as the most devastating possible act: taking what Israel valued most as YHWH's own possession and subjecting it to destruction.
22: vasitem et asher asiti el-sefam lo tachpehu velechem anashim lo tochelu (waʿăśîtem kĕʾăšer ʿāśîtî ʾel-śāpām lōʾ tĕḥāp̄û wĕleḥem ʾănāšîm lōʾ tōʾkēlû): Israel will do what Ezekiel did (no covering the lip, no eating mourner's bread) — not because they are commanded to, but because the scale of disaster will be beyond normal mourning. The prophet's sign-act anticipates a national paralysis of grief: too many dead, too rapid a collapse, too overwhelming a loss for the normal ritual of mourning to contain.
23: ufearekhem al-roshekhem venaaleykhem beragleykhem lo tispadu velo tivku unemastem baavonoteykhem venehamtem ish el-achiv (ûp̄iʾarêkem ʿal-rāʾšêkem wĕnaʿălêkem bĕraglêkem lōʾ tispĕdû wĕlōʾ tibkû ûnĕmaqqôtem baʿăwōnōtêkem wĕnĕhāmtem ʾîš ʾel-ʾāḥîw): Unemastem baavonoteykhem (you will waste away/rot in your iniquities) — the verb maqaq (to melt, rot, waste away) is used elsewhere for physical decay (Isa 34:4; Zech 14:12). Here it is the spiritual and communal rotting that comes not from the disaster itself but from the accumulated iniquity that generated it. The mourning that cannot be publicly enacted will be processed privately in the recognition of deserved judgment.
24: veEzekiel lahem lemofet kekol asher asa taasuun behavo tideu ki ani Adonai YHWH (wĕyĕḥezqēʾl lākem lĕmôp̄ēt kĕkōl ʾăšer-ʿāśāh taʿăśûn bĕbōʾô tēdĕʿû kî ʾănî ʾădōnāy Yhwh): Mofet (מוֹפֵת, sign/wonder/portent) — the same word used for the miraculous signs of the Exodus (Deut 4:34; 6:22; 7:19). Ezekiel is himself a sign of the caliber of the Exodus signs: his life enacts prophetically what YHWH is about to do nationally. The recognition formula (tideu ki ani Adonai YHWH) is the climax: when the disaster matches the sign, Israel will know who YHWH truly is.
25: veata ben-adam halo beyom kekhti meyhem et-mazuzam gaon ezam machmad eineyhem umasha naphsham et-beneyhem veet-benoteyhem (wĕʾattāh ben-ʾādām hălōʾ bĕyôm qeḥtî mēhem ʾet-mĕʿûzzām śimḥat tip̄ʾartām maḥmad ʿênêhem ûmašʾat naphšām bĕnêhem ûbĕnôtêhem): The accumulation of terms for what Israel valued most — mazon ezam (their stronghold), simchat tifartam (the joy of their glory), machmad eineyhem (the desire of their eyes), mashat naphsham (the longing of their soul) — and then “their sons and daughters” — piles up what will be taken. The temple, the children: everything. The day of maximum loss is the day that will finally produce true recognition of YHWH.
26: beyom hahu yavo eleyha hapaleit lehashmia etneyikh (bĕyôm hahûʾ yābōʾ ʾēlêhā happālîṭ lĕhašmîaʿ ʾoznêkā): Hapaleit (the fugitive/escapee) — this is the escapee who brings news (cf. Ezek 33:21 where the fugitive arrives). The coming of the fugitive signals the end of Ezekiel's prophetic dumbness (v. 27). The structural function: the prophet's silence corresponds to the silence of God's patience; when judgment falls and the fugitive arrives, both the prophet and the message are released.
27: beyom hahu yipatach piykha et-hapaleit vetidaber velo teaelem od vehayita lahem lemofet vedau ki ani YHWH (bĕyôm hahûʾ yippātaḥ pîkā ʾet-hāpālîṭ wĕdibbartā wĕlōʾ tēʾālēm ʿôd wĕhāyîtā lāhem lĕmôp̄ēt wĕyādĕʿû kî ʾănî Yhwh): Yipatach piykha (your mouth will be opened) — Ezekiel has been dumb/restricted in speech since 3:26 (“I will make your tongue cling to the roof of your mouth”). This verse announces the end of that restriction when the news of Jerusalem's fall arrives. The prophet's muteness mirrored the silence of divine patience; the opening of his mouth will mirror the outpouring of YHWH's restorative speech in chs. 33-48. The entire prophetic program pivots on this verse.
